testingRequired.title: Water Testing Recommended
testingRequired.body: Water appearance does not indicate safety. Many contaminants are colourless, odourless, and tasteless — including lead, arsenic, nitrates, and most pathogens at typical concentrations. Water testing by a certified laboratory is recommended before selecting a treatment system.

surfaceWaterRisks.title: Surface Water Requires Multiple Treatment Stages
surfaceWaterRisks.body: Surface water (rivers, lakes, ponds) may contain biological, agricultural, and industrial contamination simultaneously. A single treatment method is unlikely to be adequate. Multiple treatment stages are typically required for safe drinking water.

maintenanceRequired.title: Treatment Systems Require Regular Maintenance
maintenanceRequired.body: All treatment systems require maintenance. Expired filter cartridges, fouled membranes, and depleted UV lamps do not provide protection and may give false assurance. Improperly maintained systems can increase health risk by providing a false sense of safety.

clearWaterFallacy.title: Clear Water Is Not Necessarily Safe Water
clearWaterFallacy.body: Many of the most harmful contaminants — lead, arsenic, nitrates, PFAS, uranium — are completely undetectable by sight, smell, or taste at dangerous concentrations. Visually clear, odourless water can carry severe long-term health risks. Never assume water is safe because it looks clean. Testing by a certified laboratory is the only reliable way to assess chemical and radiological risk.

treatmentTrainOrder.title: The Order of Treatment Stages Is Critical
treatmentTrainOrder.body: Filtration must always precede disinfection: UV light cannot penetrate turbid water; chlorine is consumed by organic matter before it can kill pathogens; reverse osmosis membranes are permanently damaged by free chlorine. Always pre-filter before disinfecting. Running the stages in the wrong order wastes chemicals, destroys equipment, and leaves pathogens in the water.

recontaminationStorage.title: Treated Water Can Be Re-Contaminated During Storage
recontaminationStorage.body: Correctly treated water can be re-contaminated during transfer or storage in dirty containers. Always store treated water in a clean, covered container with a narrow opening. Never insert hands or utensils directly into the storage vessel — use a clean ladle or tap. Re-treating does not help if the storage container is the source of contamination.

exhaustedFilters.title: Expired Filters May Be More Dangerous Than No Filter
exhaustedFilters.body: An exhausted activated carbon filter adsorbs nothing and may release previously captured contaminants. A cracked ceramic element can allow pathogens through while looking intact. An ageing UV lamp may deliver insufficient dose. A poorly maintained treatment system creates false assurance and can be more dangerous than no system at all. Replace all consumables on the manufacturer's schedule, not when they visibly deteriorate.

acuteVsChronicRisk.title: Biological and Chemical Risks Have Different Urgency
acuteVsChronicRisk.body: Biological contamination (bacteria, viruses, protozoa) causes illness within hours to days — it requires immediate action. Chemical contamination (arsenic, lead, nitrates, PFAS) typically causes harm only after months or years of regular exposure — it is serious but not an emergency that requires immediately abandoning your water source. Understanding this distinction prevents both dangerous inaction on biological risks and unnecessary panic about chemical ones.

vulnerablePopulations.title: Some Groups Face Much Higher Risk
vulnerablePopulations.body: Infants under 6 months: nitrates and nitrites can cause fatal blue baby syndrome at concentrations safe for adults — never use water above 10 mg/L NO₃-N (44 mg/L NO₃⁻) for infant formula or food. Pregnant women: lead and methylmercury cross the placenta and cause irreversible neurological damage to the developing foetus — no safe level exists. Immunocompromised individuals (HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy, organ transplant): Cryptosporidium can be life-threatening — only absolute physical barriers (RO, ≤1 µm absolute filters) or boiling reliably protect this group. Kidney dialysis patients: chloramines and aluminium must be completely absent from dialysis water — standard drinking water treatment is insufficient for dialysis use.

bacteria.name: Bacteria
bacteria.description: Harmful bacteria including E. coli and Salmonella are a leading cause of waterborne illness worldwide.
bacteria.sources: Open defecation and sewage overflow near water sources are the primary causes. Animal waste from livestock near wells and streams, flooding that spreads faecal material, and untreated groundwater in areas without sanitation infrastructure are significant contributors.
bacteria.healthRisks: Causes severe gastrointestinal illness — diarrhoea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, and fever. Can be fatal in young children, elderly people, and immunocompromised individuals. E. coli O157:H7 can cause haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS) with kidney failure. Typhoid fever (Salmonella typhi) and cholera (Vibrio cholerae) remain major causes of death in regions with poor sanitation.
bacteria.detection: Field test strips can detect total coliform and E. coli within hours. Laboratory culture analysis confirms specific pathogen species. Assume bacterial contamination in any untreated surface water or groundwater where sanitation infrastructure is inadequate, especially after flooding.

viruses.name: Viruses
viruses.description: Waterborne viruses including Hepatitis A, Norovirus, and Rotavirus spread through faecally contaminated water.
viruses.sources: Human sewage contamination is the primary source. Flooding, inadequate sanitation near wells, and asymptomatic infected individuals contaminating shared water sources spread viral contamination rapidly. Viruses are far smaller than bacteria and pass through many filters that stop bacteria.
viruses.healthRisks: Hepatitis A causes liver inflammation and jaundice lasting weeks to months. Norovirus and Rotavirus cause acute vomiting and diarrhoea leading to dangerous dehydration, particularly in children under five. Polio virus, though largely eradicated, spreads via contaminated water in under-vaccinated populations.
viruses.detection: Standard field test strips do not detect viruses. Specialised laboratory PCR or immunoassay testing is required. Treat all untreated surface water as potentially contaminated with viruses — they survive longer than bacteria and require higher disinfection doses.

protozoa.name: Protozoa (Giardia, Cryptosporidium)
protozoa.description: Parasitic protozoa form hardy cysts resistant to chlorine, making physical filtration essential for their removal.
protozoa.sources: Animal and human faeces contaminating surface water are the primary source. Livestock grazing near water bodies, irrigation runoff, and wildlife are reservoir hosts. Cysts survive for months in cold water and resist standard chlorine doses — they are among the most chlorine-resistant waterborne pathogens.
protozoa.healthRisks: Giardia causes prolonged diarrhoea, cramps, bloating, and weight loss lasting weeks to months. Cryptosporidiosis causes watery diarrhoea with severe dehydration and can be life-threatening or chronic in immunocompromised individuals (including HIV/AIDS patients). Cryptosporidium outbreaks have occurred in chlorinated municipal water supplies that lacked adequate filtration.
protozoa.detection: Microscopic examination or immunofluorescence (IFA) testing by a laboratory. Field tests are unreliable. Assume protozoan contamination in any untreated river, lake, pond, or surface water — and in chlorinated water that has not been filtered, as cysts are not reliably killed by standard chlorine doses.

cyanobacteria.name: Cyanobacteria (Blue-Green Algae)
cyanobacteria.description: Photosynthetic bacteria that form toxic blooms in warm, nutrient-rich water bodies, producing cyanotoxins hazardous to humans and animals.
cyanobacteria.sources: Nutrient pollution from agricultural runoff (nitrogen and phosphorus from fertilisers) drives bloom formation in lakes, reservoirs, and slow-moving rivers. Warm temperatures and stagnant conditions accelerate growth. Blooms are becoming more frequent due to climate change and intensified agriculture worldwide.
cyanobacteria.healthRisks: Cyanobacteria cells contain cyanotoxins (see Cyanotoxins entry). Cell contact causes skin irritation and eye inflammation. Ingestion of bloom water causes gastroenteritis. Dogs and livestock are particularly vulnerable and deaths have been reported after swimming in or drinking from bloom-affected water. Not all blooms are toxigenic, but distinguishing them requires laboratory testing.
cyanobacteria.detection: Visible green, blue-green, white, or brown discolouration and surface scum. A musty, earthy, or grassy odour may be present. Laboratory PCR testing or microscopy identifies species; ELISA or HPLC tests confirm toxin levels. Never drink or use water with a visible algal bloom without treatment.

cyanotoxins.name: Cyanotoxins (Microcystin, Cylindrospermopsin)
cyanotoxins.description: Toxins released by cyanobacteria that persist as dissolved compounds in water even after bacterial cells are destroyed.
cyanotoxins.sources: Released when cyanobacterial cells lyse — which occurs naturally during cell death, or can be triggered by copper sulphate treatment, UV exposure, or oxidative disinfection. Dissolved cyanotoxins may be present in water even when no visible bloom is apparent or after bloom water has been treated.
cyanotoxins.healthRisks: Microcystin-LR is an IARC Group 2B possible human carcinogen and causes acute liver damage at high doses. Cylindrospermopsin damages liver and kidneys. Anatoxin-a is a fast-acting neurotoxin. The WHO provisional guideline for microcystin-LR in drinking water is 1 µg/L. Standard water treatment by boiling does not destroy cyanotoxins.
cyanotoxins.detection: Standard water quality tests do not detect cyanotoxins. ELISA immunoassay kits and HPLC laboratory analysis are required. Testing is recommended after any visible bloom event and for water abstracted from lakes and reservoirs during summer months in temperate regions.

turbidity.name: Turbidity
turbidity.description: A measure of water cloudiness caused by suspended particles — an important indicator of treatment need and filtration effectiveness.
turbidity.sources: Soil erosion and agricultural runoff during rainfall, disturbed sediment from flooding, algae blooms, fine clay particles, and organic decomposition. Seasonally elevated in rivers after heavy rainfall and snowmelt. Turbidity is a measure of particle load rather than a contaminant itself — sediment, silt, and biological material are the underlying causes.
turbidity.healthRisks: Suspended particles shield and protect pathogens from disinfection by UV light and chlorine, making turbid water significantly harder to treat safely. High turbidity is a strong indicator of faecal contamination risk. The WHO guideline for treated drinking water is below 1 NTU to ensure effective disinfection.
turbidity.detection: Visually apparent at high levels — cloudy, brown, or grey water. Turbidity meters measure in NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units). A simple visual test: fill a clear 30 cm glass column — if newspaper print cannot be read through it, turbidity is too high for safe direct disinfection.

sediment.name: Sediment, Sand & Silt
sediment.description: Suspended inorganic particles that reduce water clarity, damage equipment, and carry adsorbed chemical contaminants.
sediment.sources: Soil erosion during rainfall, riverbank erosion, disturbed riverbeds, road construction near water sources, flooding, and agricultural cultivation near water bodies. Seasonal peaks occur after storms and snowmelt. Sand and grit damage pump impellers and membrane systems if not pre-filtered.
sediment.healthRisks: Sediment itself has low direct toxicity but provides attachment surfaces for bacteria, viruses, and heavy metals, protecting them from disinfection. Fine clay particles carry adsorbed agricultural chemicals including pesticides and herbicides. High sediment loads reduce the effectiveness of UV disinfection and chlorination and can clog and damage downstream filter components.
sediment.detection: Visual inspection — cloudy, murky, or gritty water. Settle a water sample in a clear container for 30 minutes and observe deposits. Turbidity meter measurement in NTU provides a quantitative assessment. Pre-filtration is required whenever visible sediment is present.

microplastics.name: Microplastics
microplastics.description: Plastic fragments and fibres smaller than 5 mm, increasingly detected in drinking water sources and treated municipal water worldwide.
microplastics.sources: Degradation of larger plastic waste in rivers and oceans, synthetic textile washing releasing fibres, road surface wear from tyre particles, industrial plastic processing, and cosmetic microbeads (now banned in many countries). Found in surface water, groundwater, and treated tap water — their ubiquity reflects global plastic pollution.
microplastics.healthRisks: Health effects are under active scientific investigation. WHO (2019) concluded current evidence does not indicate a risk at concentrations detected in drinking water, but called for further research. Microplastics can carry adsorbed chemical pollutants (PCBs, pesticides) and may facilitate their transfer to tissue. Long-term effects of chronic low-level ingestion are unknown and cannot be excluded.
microplastics.detection: Requires laboratory analysis — microscopy (stereomicroscopy, SEM) or spectroscopy (FTIR or Raman spectroscopy). No reliable field test kits are available. Standard water quality tests do not detect microplastics. Concentrations in tap water are typically below 100 particles per litre.

lead.name: Lead
lead.description: A toxic heavy metal that leaches into drinking water primarily from lead service pipes, lead solder, and lead-containing brass fittings in older plumbing systems.
lead.sources: Lead service lines (installed before 1986–1990 in most developed countries), lead-tin solder in copper plumbing, and lead-containing brass fittings are the primary sources. Lead is rarely present in source water itself — contamination predominantly occurs within the distribution system and household plumbing. Soft, acidic, and stagnant water conditions increase lead leaching significantly.
lead.healthRisks: There is no safe level of lead exposure in children. Lead causes irreversible neurological damage, reduced IQ, behavioural problems, and developmental delays — with the greatest harm during fetal development and early childhood. In adults, chronic exposure elevates blood pressure, is linked to kidney disease and cardiovascular events. WHO guideline: 10 µg/L; US EPA action level: 15 µg/L, with a health goal of zero.
lead.detection: Cannot be detected by taste, smell, or appearance. Requires laboratory analysis (ICP-MS gold standard) or certified home test kits. Flush the tap for 30 seconds before sampling to avoid first-draw lead concentrations from standing water in pipes. All homes with pre-1986 plumbing or lead service lines should be tested.

arsenic.name: Arsenic
arsenic.description: A naturally occurring metalloid that contaminates groundwater through geological dissolution and industrial activity, affecting over 140 million people worldwide.
arsenic.sources: Natural weathering of arsenic-rich rock formations is the primary source — particularly severe in South and Southeast Asia (Bangladesh, India, Myanmar, Vietnam), parts of Latin America (Chile, Argentina), and sub-Saharan Africa. Mining operations, coal combustion, and historical use of arsenical pesticides are anthropogenic sources.
arsenic.healthRisks: Arsenic is an IARC Group 1 human carcinogen. Chronic exposure via drinking water causes cancers of the bladder, lung, kidney, and skin. Non-cancerous effects include peripheral neuropathy, skin lesions (arsenical keratosis), and cardiovascular disease. WHO guideline: 10 µg/L. In severely affected regions, natural concentrations can exceed 1000 µg/L. The 10 µg/L WHO and EPA guideline is a risk-management compromise based on analytical and treatment feasibility — not a safe threshold. At 10 µg/L, long-term exposure still carries a measurable excess cancer risk. Treatment is recommended for any detectable arsenic, not only for levels exceeding the guideline.
arsenic.detection: Arsenic is odourless and tasteless at all relevant concentrations. Colourimetric field test kits are available with sensitivity to approximately 10–50 µg/L; laboratory ICP-MS testing is required for regulatory compliance. Communities in endemic geological regions should test all drinking water sources.

mercury.name: Mercury
mercury.description: A toxic heavy metal occurring in elemental, inorganic, and organic (methylmercury) forms, with methylmercury being the most hazardous for human health.
mercury.sources: Industrial point-source discharges are the primary route of mercury contamination in drinking water: chlor-alkali plants, artisanal and small-scale gold mining using mercury amalgamation, coal combustion, and waste incineration. Natural degassing from volcanic activity and mercury-bearing geological formations contributes background levels. Important distinction: methylmercury — the form most associated with severe neurological harm — accumulates in fish via aquatic food chains and represents a dietary exposure pathway unrelated to drinking water. In drinking water, inorganic mercury from industrial sources is the primary concern. Standard water testing for mercury measures inorganic forms.
mercury.healthRisks: Methylmercury is a potent neurotoxin that readily crosses the blood-brain barrier and placenta. Prenatal exposure causes developmental delays and neurological damage in children. Inorganic mercury affects kidneys and the central nervous system. WHO guideline for inorganic mercury in drinking water: 6 µg/L.
mercury.detection: Cannot be detected by taste, smell, or appearance at guideline levels. Requires laboratory analysis — cold vapour atomic absorption spectroscopy or ICP-MS. Standard field tests are unreliable. Testing is recommended near industrial facilities, mining sites, and in artisanal gold mining communities.

cadmium.name: Cadmium
cadmium.description: A heavy metal that accumulates in the kidneys over decades, primarily entering water through phosphate fertiliser use and industrial contamination.
cadmium.sources: Phosphate fertilisers contain cadmium as a geological impurity — agricultural runoff is a significant diffuse source. Zinc smelting, battery manufacturing, and electroplating operations discharge cadmium into water bodies. Some galvanised pipes and plumbing materials may also leach cadmium.
cadmium.healthRisks: Cadmium is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Chronic low-level exposure accumulates in the kidneys over decades, eventually causing irreversible kidney damage (cadmium nephropathy). Associated with increased risk of lung and prostate cancer. Bone damage (Itai-itai disease) occurred at very high industrial exposures in Japan. WHO guideline: 3 µg/L.
cadmium.detection: Cadmium is colourless, odourless, and tasteless at all relevant concentrations. Laboratory ICP-MS analysis is required for detection below WHO guideline levels. Field test kits have limited sensitivity. Testing is recommended in agricultural areas with intensive fertiliser use and near industrial facilities.

chromium_6.name: Chromium-6
chromium_6.description: The toxic hexavalent form of chromium used in industrial processes — more mobile and carcinogenic than trivalent chromium (Cr-III).
chromium_6.sources: Industrial discharge from chrome plating, leather tanning, and dye manufacturing. Improper disposal of chromate-containing waste. Natural geological sources in ultramafic rock regions. Chromium-6 contamination affects water systems in industrial regions of China, India, and parts of the United States (notably Hinkley, California).
chromium_6.healthRisks: Chromium-6 is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Ingestion is linked to stomach cancer in epidemiological studies. Occupational inhalation causes lung cancer. The WHO and US EPA MCL of 100 µg/L as total chromium is considered inadequate by many researchers given the evidence for ingestion risks. California's Public Health Goal for Cr-VI is 0.02 µg/L.
chromium_6.detection: Requires laboratory testing — colorimetric diphenylcarbazide method distinguishes Cr-VI from total chromium. ICP-MS with speciation measures both forms. Standard total chromium tests do not identify the toxic Cr-VI form. Testing is recommended near industrial facilities and in geological regions with ultramafic rock.

copper.name: Copper
copper.description: A metal that leaches from copper plumbing into drinking water, especially in soft or acidic water — detectable by a metallic taste at elevated concentrations.
copper.sources: Corrosion of copper pipes and fittings is the primary source in treated water distribution systems. Copper is rarely a concern in natural source water. Soft, acidic, or low-pH water accelerates copper leaching — standing water in copper pipes overnight concentrates copper significantly. Agricultural fungicides (copper sulphate) can contaminate surface water.
copper.healthRisks: Acute exposure at high concentrations (above 1–2 mg/L) causes nausea, vomiting, and gastrointestinal irritation. Chronic exposure at lower levels can cause liver and kidney damage over time. Children with Wilson's disease (inherited copper metabolism disorder) are particularly susceptible. WHO guideline: 2 mg/L. Copper is detectable as a metallic taste above approximately 1 mg/L.
copper.detection: Colorimetric test kits and test strips for copper are widely available and reliable. Laboratory analysis provides more accurate quantification. First-draw samples after overnight stagnation are most likely to show elevated levels. Running the tap for 30 seconds in the morning reduces first-draw copper concentrations.

chlorine.name: Chlorine & Disinfection By-products
chlorine.description: Residual chlorine from municipal treatment and the trihalomethanes (THMs) it forms when reacting with organic matter.
chlorine.sources: Chlorine is intentionally added by municipal water treatment as a disinfectant and is regulated. Levels may be elevated after treatment plant interventions. Disinfection by-products (DBPs) — trihalomethanes and haloacetic acids — form when chlorine reacts with natural organic matter in the source water.
chlorine.healthRisks: Residual chlorine causes unpleasant taste and odour above approximately 0.3 mg/L. More significantly, trihalomethanes (THMs) including chloroform are classified as probable human carcinogens (IARC Group 2A). Haloacetic acids (HAAs) are also regulated and linked to increased bladder cancer risk with decades of exposure. The relative risk remains low at regulated levels, but reducing DBP exposure is prudent.
chlorine.detection: Inexpensive DPD colorimetric test strips and drop test kits are widely available and reliable for free chlorine. Detectable by smell at concentrations above 0.3 mg/L. Municipal water quality reports disclose chlorine and THM levels. THMs require laboratory analysis (GC-MS) for accurate measurement.

nitrates.name: Nitrates
nitrates.description: High nitrate levels from agricultural fertilisers are particularly dangerous for infants under six months.
nitrates.sources: Agricultural fertiliser runoff is the dominant source, especially in regions with intensive arable farming. Septic system leakage, animal feedlots, and microbial decomposition of organic matter also contribute. Nitrate is highly soluble and mobile in groundwater — it persists for decades after the original contamination event.
nitrates.healthRisks: Nitrates are converted to nitrite in the body, which oxidises haemoglobin to methaemoglobin, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity. In infants under six months, this causes methaemoglobinaemia (blue baby syndrome), which can be rapidly fatal. Long-term adult exposure at elevated levels has been linked to increased colorectal cancer risk in some epidemiological studies. WHO guideline: 50 mg/L (as NO₃⁻); US EPA MCL: 10 mg/L as nitrogen. Important unit note: WHO and EPA express nitrate limits in different units but they are nearly equivalent — WHO 50 mg/L as NO₃⁻ equals approximately 11 mg/L as nitrogen (NO₃-N); US EPA 10 mg/L as nitrogen equals 44 mg/L as NO₃⁻. Always confirm which unit your test kit or laboratory report uses before comparing results to either guideline.
nitrates.detection: Nitrate test strips are widely available at aquarium and pool supply stores and are reliably accurate. Laboratory ion chromatography provides precise regulatory-compliance measurements. The WHO guideline of 50 mg/L applies to adults; infant safety requires levels below 10 mg/L (as NO₃-N). Always confirm whether your result is in mg/L NO₃⁻ or mg/L nitrogen — these differ by a factor of 4.4 (multiply mg/L NO₃-N × 4.4 to obtain mg/L NO₃⁻). Misreading units can lead to a 4× error in safety assessment.

nitrites.name: Nitrites
nitrites.description: A reactive nitrogen compound more acutely toxic than nitrates at equivalent concentrations — its presence in drinking water often indicates microbiological activity.
nitrites.sources: Microbial conversion of nitrates by bacteria in distribution pipes or stored water. Incompletely treated sewage and organic matter decomposition. Nitrites are an intermediate in the nitrogen cycle and rarely persist in well-oxygenated groundwater — their presence in finished water often signals bacterial activity or sewage contamination.
nitrites.healthRisks: Nitrites directly convert haemoglobin to methaemoglobin, reducing blood oxygen-carrying capacity — more acutely toxic than nitrates because the conversion step already occurred. Most dangerous for infants under six months (blue baby syndrome). WHO guideline: 3 mg/L. Elevated nitrites in finished water may also indicate bacterial contamination that warrants investigation.
nitrites.detection: Nitrite test strips are widely available and sensitive at relevant concentrations. Laboratory ion chromatography provides accurate measurement. Any detectable nitrite in finished drinking water warrants investigation of source contamination or distribution system bacterial growth.

fluoride.name: Fluoride
fluoride.description: Excess fluoride from geological sources causes dental and skeletal fluorosis — a problem in specific regions worldwide.
fluoride.sources: Natural volcanic rock formations and sedimentary deposits leach fluoride into groundwater — a major problem in the East African Rift Valley, the Indian subcontinent, and parts of China. Industrial emissions from fertiliser manufacturing and aluminium smelting also contribute. In contrast, some municipal water systems deliberately add fluoride at low concentrations (0.7 mg/L) to prevent tooth decay.
fluoride.healthRisks: At levels above 1.5 mg/L (WHO guideline), fluoride causes dental fluorosis — mottling and weakening of tooth enamel. At levels above 4 mg/L, skeletal fluorosis develops, causing joint pain, ligament calcification, and progressive bone damage that can cause crippling deformity in severe endemic cases. The range between beneficial and harmful concentrations is narrow.
fluoride.detection: Requires laboratory analysis or specialised SPADNS photometric test kits for accurate results. Standard field test strips have limited accuracy for fluoride. Geological maps and regional surveys of endemic fluoride areas provide useful preliminary information about risk.

pesticides.name: Pesticides
pesticides.description: A broad class of synthetic chemicals used in agriculture to control insects, fungi, and other pests — many are persistent and toxic at low concentrations in water.
pesticides.sources: Agricultural runoff following rainfall on treated fields is the primary pathway to surface water. Direct surface water contamination from spray drift. Leaching through soil into groundwater — particularly from older, more persistent organochlorine compounds. Urban pesticide use in parks and gardens contributes to stormwater contamination.
pesticides.healthRisks: Health effects vary widely by compound. Organochlorines (DDT, endrin, dieldrin) are persistent bioaccumulative toxicants linked to endocrine disruption and cancer. Organophosphates (chlorpyrifos, malathion) inhibit acetylcholinesterase, affecting the nervous system acutely. Many pesticides are classified as possible or probable human carcinogens by IARC. WHO individual compound guidelines range from 0.1 µg/L to 100 µg/L.
pesticides.detection: No single field test detects all pesticide classes. GC-MS or LC-MS laboratory analysis is required for comprehensive multi-residue screening. Targeted immunoassay field kits exist for specific compounds (atrazine, glyphosate, chlorpyrifos). Regular testing is recommended for water sources in agricultural catchments, particularly after rainfall events.

herbicides.name: Herbicides
herbicides.description: Plant-killing chemicals used to control weeds in agriculture and urban areas, which contaminate surface and groundwater particularly after rainfall.
herbicides.sources: Application to cropland, roadsides, railway corridors, and urban green spaces. Runoff during rainfall events is the primary pathway to surface water. Atrazine, glyphosate, and 2,4-D are among the most frequently detected herbicides in water worldwide. Some compounds are highly persistent in groundwater.
herbicides.healthRisks: Glyphosate is classified by IARC as Group 2A (probable human carcinogen) based on animal and mechanistic evidence, though some regulatory bodies dispute this assessment. Atrazine is an endocrine disruptor with documented effects on amphibian reproductive systems. 2,4-D is associated with Non-Hodgkin lymphoma in some epidemiological studies. WHO guidelines for individual herbicides range widely.
herbicides.detection: ELISA immunoassay field kits are available for atrazine and glyphosate with reasonable sensitivity. Laboratory GC-MS or LC-MS provides comprehensive multi-residue screening. Regular monitoring is recommended for water sources in intensive agricultural areas, particularly at times of peak herbicide application.

pfas.name: PFAS (Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances)
pfas.description: A group of thousands of synthetic fluorinated chemicals used since the 1940s — now considered persistent global contaminants in water supplies worldwide.
pfas.sources: Aqueous film-forming foam (AFFF) used at military bases and airports is a major point source. Industrial manufacturing of fluoropolymers (including polytetrafluoroethylene, PTFE) and fluorosurfactants. Contaminated consumer products including non-stick cookware, food packaging, stain-resistant textiles, and firefighting foams. PFAS are detected in water systems across most industrialised nations.
pfas.healthRisks: PFOA and PFOS are classified as IARC Group 2A (probable) and 2B (possible) carcinogens respectively. Health effects include thyroid disruption, immune suppression, elevated cholesterol, and reproductive harm. PFOA is associated with kidney and testicular cancer. The US EPA set health advisory levels for PFOA and PFOS at 4 ng/L each (2024) — among the lowest water quality standards ever set. PFAS are called 'forever chemicals' because fluorine-carbon bonds do not naturally biodegrade.
pfas.detection: Requires laboratory analysis — LC-MS/MS (EPA Method 533 or 537.1) can detect a wide range of PFAS compounds at ng/L concentrations. No reliable field test kits are currently available. Testing is recommended near military airports, industrial facilities with known PFAS use, and in areas with documented PFAS groundwater contamination. Treatment: reverse osmosis removes over 90% of PFAS and is the most reliable household option. High-contact-time granular activated carbon (GAC) significantly reduces PFAS but requires frequent media replacement; standard carbon block pitcher filters provide limited protection. Nanofiltration is also effective.

vocs.name: Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)
vocs.description: Carbon-based chemicals that evaporate readily at room temperature, including industrial solvents, fuel components, and chlorination by-products.
vocs.sources: Leaking underground fuel storage tanks (benzene, toluene, MTBE) at petrol stations and industrial facilities. Industrial solvent disposal and dry-cleaning chemical releases (tetrachloroethylene PCE, trichloroethylene TCE). Chlorination by-products (trihalomethanes including chloroform) form during water disinfection. VOCs enter groundwater through soil percolation.
vocs.healthRisks: Benzene is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen and a known cause of leukaemia. Tetrachloroethylene (PCE) and trichloroethylene (TCE) are IARC Group 1 carcinogens associated with kidney cancer and Non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Trihalomethanes including chloroform are associated with increased bladder cancer risk with long-term exposure. WHO provides individual guidelines for each compound.
vocs.detection: Many VOCs produce a petroleum or solvent odour detectable at higher concentrations, but numerous VOCs are odourless at guideline concentrations. Purge-and-trap GC-MS laboratory analysis is the standard method (US EPA Method 524.2). Passive sampler sticks and some immunoassay kits provide on-site screening for specific compounds.

petroleum.name: Petroleum & Fuel Residues
petroleum.description: Hydrocarbons from crude oil, refined fuels, and lubricants that contaminate water through spills, storage tank leaks, and industrial discharge.
petroleum.sources: Leaking underground storage tanks at petrol stations and industrial facilities. Pipeline spills and tanker accidents. Stormwater runoff from roads and car parks. Oilfield produced water disposal. Agricultural use of heavy machinery near water sources. Urban runoff from roads carries tyre and road surface hydrocarbon residues.
petroleum.healthRisks: Petroleum mixtures contain benzene (IARC Group 1 carcinogen causing leukaemia), toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene (BTEX compounds), as well as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs). Benzo[a]pyrene and other PAHs are IARC Group 1 carcinogens. Petroleum in water is detectable by visible sheen, odour, and taste at very low concentrations — often below health-relevant thresholds.
petroleum.detection: Visible oily sheen on water surface and petroleum odour are detectable at low concentrations. Total petroleum hydrocarbon (TPH) laboratory analysis provides quantification. BTEX compounds are measured separately by GC-MS (EPA Method 602/524). Rapid field immunoassay kits are available for some compounds.

hardness.name: Hardness (Calcium & Magnesium)
hardness.description: The dissolved calcium and magnesium content of water, responsible for scale formation and reduced soap lathering — not a health hazard at typical levels.
hardness.sources: Natural dissolution of limestone (calcium carbonate) and dolomite (magnesium carbonate) as groundwater passes through rock formations. Hard water is most common in regions with chalk and limestone geology (southern England, central Europe, parts of the Middle East). Not a health hazard — calcium and magnesium are essential dietary minerals.
hardness.healthRisks: Hard water is not a health concern and may provide beneficial dietary calcium and magnesium. Scale (calcium carbonate) deposits in pipes, boilers, water heaters, and appliances reduce energy efficiency and lifespan. Very soft water (low mineral content) is more corrosive and may leach lead and copper from plumbing more readily. WHO does not set a health-based guideline for hardness.
hardness.detection: Standard hardness test strips are widely available and accurate. Results expressed in mg/L CaCO₃ or degrees of hardness (°dH). Soft: below 60 mg/L; moderate: 60–120 mg/L; hard: 120–180 mg/L; very hard: above 180 mg/L. Practical indicators include soap scum formation, kettle scale, and reduced washing machine efficiency.

iron.name: Iron
iron.description: A naturally occurring metal that causes orange-brown staining, metallic taste, and supports bacterial growth in water at elevated concentrations.
iron.sources: Natural dissolution from iron-rich geological formations is the primary source — common in groundwater from wells in areas with iron-bearing rock. Corrosion of unlined steel and cast iron pipes in distribution systems adds iron in older infrastructure. Anaerobic (oxygen-depleted) groundwater typically contains more dissolved ferrous iron than surface water.
iron.healthRisks: Iron is an essential nutrient. WHO does not set a health-based guideline but sets an aesthetic guideline of 0.3 mg/L. Elevated iron causes rust-orange staining of laundry, porcelain, and plumbing fixtures, metallic taste, and promotes growth of iron-oxidising bacteria in pipes (which form slime deposits). Very high levels above 1 mg/L may indicate other groundwater quality issues.
iron.detection: Orange-brown discolouration and metallic taste are practical indicators. Colourimetric iron test strips and kits are reliable and widely available. Note the distinction between ferrous iron (dissolved, water appears clear when drawn) and ferric iron (oxidised, rust-coloured particles visible).

manganese.name: Manganese
manganese.description: A naturally occurring metal that causes black staining and may impair neurological development at elevated concentrations.
manganese.sources: Geological dissolution from manganese-containing sedimentary and metamorphic rock, particularly in deep anaerobic groundwater. Industrial discharges from steel manufacturing, battery production, and mining. Often co-occurs with iron in reducing groundwater conditions.
manganese.healthRisks: Manganese is an essential trace nutrient but neurotoxic at elevated chronic exposures. WHO updated its provisional guideline to 0.08 mg/L (2022) following evidence linking chronic exposure to reduced cognitive function in children. Studies from Bangladesh and Canada have shown IQ deficits and neurological effects in children exposed to drinking water above 100–300 µg/L. Black or dark-brown staining of fixtures occurs above 0.1 mg/L.
manganese.detection: Colourimetric test kits and strips are available for manganese, though sensitivity varies between products. Laboratory ICP-MS provides accurate quantification. Black or dark-brown staining in toilet cisterns, sinks, and laundry is a practical indicator at elevated levels.

salinity.name: High Salinity / Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)
salinity.description: Elevated dissolved salt content that makes water unpalatable for drinking and unsuitable for irrigation — a growing problem in coastal and arid regions.
salinity.sources: Natural geological sources including evaporite deposits (halite, gypsum) and marine sedimentary formations. Seawater intrusion into coastal aquifers due to over-extraction. Agricultural drainage with irrigation-concentrated salts and fertiliser residues. Industrial brine discharge. Climate-driven drought concentrating dissolved solids in surface water and shallow aquifers.
salinity.healthRisks: WHO aesthetic guideline for TDS is 600 mg/L, with an upper acceptability limit of 1000 mg/L. Drinking water above 2000 mg/L TDS causes osmotic diarrhoea. At sodium concentrations above 200 mg/L, there is increased risk of hypertension in sodium-sensitive individuals. Highly saline water is corrosive to metal plumbing and unsuitable for irrigation of most crops.
salinity.detection: TDS meters are inexpensive and widely available — dip and read results in mg/L or ppm. Electrical conductivity meters measure a closely related parameter. A distinctly salty or brackish taste is detectable above approximately 600 mg/L. Laboratory ionic analysis identifies specific dissolved minerals.

hydrogen_sulfide.name: Hydrogen Sulfide
hydrogen_sulfide.description: A dissolved gas producing a distinctive rotten-egg odour — primarily an aesthetic concern in drinking water but corrosive to plumbing.
hydrogen_sulfide.sources: Microbial reduction of sulphate by sulphate-reducing bacteria in anaerobic groundwater. Volcanic and geothermal sources. Industrial discharges from petroleum refining and paper manufacturing. Natural organic matter decomposition in deep anaerobic aquifers. A common complaint with private well water in areas with sulphate-rich geology.
hydrogen_sulfide.healthRisks: At concentrations detectable in drinking water (typically below 1 mg/L), hydrogen sulfide is primarily an aesthetic problem causing objectionable odour and taste. It is corrosive to copper and silver plumbing. At higher dissolved concentrations from industrial sources, it is toxic, but these levels are not normally encountered in drinking water supplies. WHO does not set a health-based guideline; odour is detectable above 0.05 mg/L.
hydrogen_sulfide.detection: Detectable by its unmistakable rotten-egg smell at concentrations above 0.05 mg/L. No reliable field test strips for dissolved H₂S — the gas degasses rapidly on exposure to air, making measurement unreliable without immediate preservation. Laboratory analysis requires rapid processing with a specific sulphide-fixing preservative.

pharmaceuticals.name: Pharmaceutical Residues
pharmaceuticals.description: Trace concentrations of medicines excreted by humans and animals, incompletely removed by conventional wastewater treatment.
pharmaceuticals.sources: Human excretion of parent compounds and metabolites of antibiotics, antidepressants, anti-epileptics, pain relievers, and other medicines. Improper disposal of unused medicines into toilets and drains. Veterinary pharmaceuticals from intensive livestock operations. Wastewater treatment plants remove most but not all pharmaceutical compounds — effluents discharge to rivers used as drinking water sources downstream.
pharmaceuticals.healthRisks: Most pharmaceuticals are present in ng/L concentrations in treated water — far below therapeutic doses. Long-term health effects of chronic low-level exposure are uncertain. Antibiotic residues may contribute to the development and spread of antimicrobial resistance, a serious public health concern. Endocrine-active compounds may have hormonal effects at very low concentrations. WHO has reviewed available evidence and has not established guideline values for most compounds at detected levels.
pharmaceuticals.detection: Requires sophisticated laboratory analysis — LC-MS/MS methods with low ng/L detection limits. No field tests are available. Not detected by standard water quality tests. Research monitoring programmes detect pharmaceuticals in source water and treated drinking water worldwide.

hormones.name: Hormones & Endocrine Disruptors
hormones.description: Natural and synthetic hormones and hormone-mimicking industrial chemicals that interfere with endocrine signalling even at very low concentrations.
hormones.sources: Natural oestrogens excreted by humans and livestock in wastewater (oestradiol, oestrone). Synthetic oestrogens from oral contraceptives (ethinyl oestradiol) are particularly potent and persistent. Industrial endocrine-disrupting chemicals including bisphenol A (BPA) and phthalates from plastics manufacturing. Some pesticides have endocrine-disrupting properties.
hormones.healthRisks: Demonstrated aquatic ecosystem effects include feminisation of fish at extremely low concentrations of ethinyl oestradiol (sub-ng/L range), with population-level reproductive impacts documented. The relevance of these concentrations for human health via drinking water is debated — most evidence suggests current exposures are below pharmacologically active thresholds. WHO does not set drinking water guidelines for most natural oestrogens. The precautionary approach supports continued monitoring.
hormones.detection: Requires highly sensitive laboratory methods — ELISA immunoassay or LC-MS/MS. Concentrations in treated drinking water are typically in the sub-ng/L to low ng/L range. Not detectable by standard water quality tests or field kits.

uranium.name: Uranium
uranium.description: A naturally occurring radioactive metal that dissolves from uranium-bearing geological formations into groundwater, acting primarily as a chemical nephrotoxin.
uranium.sources: Natural weathering of granitic and sedimentary rock containing uranium deposits — particularly significant in Finland, Scandinavia, parts of the United States Southwest, and phosphate-mining regions. Uranium ore mining and processing operations. Phosphate fertilisers contain uranium as a naturally occurring trace impurity that can leach into groundwater.
uranium.healthRisks: Uranium's primary health concern in drinking water is chemical nephrotoxicity (kidney damage) from the uranyl cation, rather than radiation. WHO guideline: 30 µg/L. Long-term exposure above guideline levels causes renal injury detectable through biomarkers. As a radioactive element, uranium also contributes to total radiation dose, though chemical toxicity typically dominates at concentrations found in drinking water.
uranium.detection: Cannot be detected by taste, smell, or appearance. Laboratory ICP-MS analysis is the standard method. Alpha spectrometry provides isotopic analysis. Radon measurement (a radium/uranium decay product) may indicate uranium-rich geology but is not a reliable proxy for uranium in water. Testing is recommended in areas with known granitic or uranium-bearing geology.

radon.name: Radon
radon.description: A naturally occurring radioactive gas produced by radium decay in soil and rock, which dissolves in groundwater and is released into indoor air during water use.
radon.sources: Decay of radium-226 from uranium-bearing rock and soil — primarily in granite, dark shale, and phosphate-rich sedimentary formations. Well water in affected geological areas can contain elevated dissolved radon. Surface water (rivers, lakes) rarely contains significant radon as it degasses rapidly into the atmosphere.
radon.healthRisks: The primary radon exposure pathway for most people is inhalation of radon gas accumulating in indoor air, not drinking water. Waterborne radon contributes approximately 2% of total radon exposure. During showering, cooking, and dishwashing, dissolved radon is released from water into indoor air, elevating concentrations. WHO reference level: 300 Bq/L in drinking water. Radon inhalation is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking (IARC Group 1).
radon.detection: Liquid scintillation counting and electret ion chambers are standard laboratory methods. Water samples must be collected without air exposure and analysed immediately (radon has a 3.8-day half-life and degasses rapidly). Specialised kits are available for well water screening. Testing is recommended for all well owners in high-radon geological areas.

radionuclides.name: Radionuclides (General Radioactivity)
radionuclides.description: A group of radioactive isotopes beyond uranium and radon that can contaminate water through natural geological sources or human industrial and nuclear activity.
radionuclides.sources: Natural radionuclides: radium-226 and radium-228 (from uranium and thorium decay chains), polonium-210, and gross alpha and beta radiation from geological sources. Anthropogenic sources: nuclear power plant discharges (tritium, cesium-137), fallout from historical nuclear weapons testing (strontium-90, plutonium), and mining waste tailings containing concentrated natural radionuclides.
radionuclides.healthRisks: Health risk depends on the isotope, concentration, and duration of exposure. Ionising radiation damages DNA and increases cancer risk proportionally with dose (no threshold). Radium-226 and Ra-228 are bone-seeking isotopes with long biological half-lives. WHO sets guidelines for total indicative dose from radionuclides at 0.1 mSv per year, with screening values for individual isotopes. Most naturally occurring radioactivity in drinking water is at low levels that represent minimal risk.
radionuclides.detection: Gross alpha and gross beta screening tests provide a first-pass indication; positive results trigger isotope-specific analysis by alpha or beta spectrometry. ICP-MS can detect uranium and other radionuclides. Monitoring is required by regulation for all public water supplies in most countries. Private wells in high-risk geological areas should be tested.

ph_low.name: Low pH (Acidic Water)
ph_low.description: Water with pH below 6.5 is corrosive and dissolves heavy metals from pipes and plumbing fixtures into the water supply.
ph_low.sources: Natural organic acids from peat or decaying vegetation, acid rain in industrial regions, CO₂ dissolution forming carbonic acid, acid mine drainage from mining sites, and naturally soft water with low buffering capacity. Distilled and reverse osmosis water is typically acidic without remineralisation.
ph_low.healthRisks: Acidic water itself is not directly toxic at drinking water pH ranges, but it aggressively leaches lead, copper, cadmium, and zinc from household plumbing. The resulting metal contamination carries significant health risks. WHO recommends pH 6.5–8.5 for drinking water. Corrosive water also deteriorates pipes and water heaters.
ph_low.detection: pH meters provide accurate real-time readings; calibrate regularly. pH test strips (4.0–8.0 range) give quick field estimates. Most water quality lab reports include pH. Check in the morning after water has sat in pipes overnight — first-flush sampling reveals worst-case corrosion effects.

ph_high.name: High pH (Alkaline Water)
ph_high.description: Water with pH above 8.5 is alkaline, affects the efficiency of disinfection, and may cause scale buildup.
ph_high.sources: Limestone and dolomite geology naturally raises groundwater pH. Concrete water mains leach lime into water. Water softening processes raise pH. Industrial discharge from cement, lime, or pulp and paper industries. Algae blooms can temporarily drive pH above 9.0 through photosynthesis.
ph_high.healthRisks: High pH water is not acutely toxic, but significantly reduces chlorine disinfection efficiency — at pH 9.0, chlorine is up to ~100× less effective than at pH 6.0. Hard alkaline water causes scale deposits in pipes and appliances. Very high pH (above 11) causes skin and eye irritation. WHO recommends pH 6.5–8.5.
ph_high.detection: pH meter or test strips. Standard water quality reports include pH measurement. Municipal systems monitor and adjust pH as part of routine treatment. For private wells, test annually or after changes in water taste or scale formation.

chloramines.name: Chloramines
chloramines.description: Chloramines are secondary disinfectants used in municipal water systems that persist longer than chlorine but are harder to remove.
chloramines.sources: Deliberately added by many municipal water utilities as a longer-lasting alternative to free chlorine. Formed when chlorine reacts with ammonia — either added intentionally or present in source water. Chloramination reduces the formation of some trihalomethanes but creates other disinfection byproducts.
chloramines.healthRisks: Chloramines can cause skin irritation and rashes in sensitive individuals. Highly dangerous for kidney dialysis patients — must be completely removed before dialysis water. Aquarium fish are highly sensitive to chloramines; standard dechlorinating products do not remove them. Some studies link long-term exposure to iodoacetic acid formation, a genotoxic chloramine byproduct.
chloramines.detection: Standard free-chlorine test strips do not detect chloramines. DPD-based total chlorine tests detect combined chlorine. Chloramine-specific test kits are available. Check your municipal water quality report — suppliers are required to disclose when chloramines are used.

disinfection_byproducts.name: Disinfection Byproducts (DBPs)
disinfection_byproducts.description: THMs and HAAs formed when chlorine or chloramines react with natural organic matter in water — a regulated but persistent public health concern.
disinfection_byproducts.sources: Formed during chlorination or chloramination when disinfectants react with naturally occurring organic matter such as humic and fulvic acids. Surface water with higher organic content produces more DBPs than groundwater. Seasonal variation is significant — warm water and higher organic loads in summer increase DBP formation. Heating chlorinated tap water (boiling, showering) may increase DBP exposure via volatilisation.
disinfection_byproducts.healthRisks: Long-term exposure to trihalomethanes (chloroform, bromodichloromethane, bromoform) is associated with increased risk of bladder cancer. Haloacetic acids (HAAs) are genotoxic in animal studies. WHO and EPA regulate total THMs at 80 µg/L and HAAs at 60 µg/L. Inhalation during showering and bathing is a significant exposure route beyond drinking.
disinfection_byproducts.detection: DBPs cannot be detected by taste or smell at regulatory levels. Laboratory analysis (GC-MS) required. Municipal water quality reports publish annual average THM and HAA levels — review your local Consumer Confidence Report. Activated carbon at the point of use reduces DBP exposure from drinking water.

sulfate.name: Sulfate
sulfate.description: Naturally occurring mineral ion that causes laxative effects at high concentrations and contributes to pipe corrosion.
sulfate.sources: Dissolution of gypsum, pyrite, and other sulfur-containing minerals in geological formations. Acid rain oxidises sulfur compounds increasing sulfate in surface water. Industrial discharge from mining, smelting, and manufacturing. Decomposition of organic matter in anaerobic conditions produces sulfate. Seawater intrusion into coastal aquifers.
sulfate.healthRisks: Concentrations above 250–500 mg/L cause a laxative effect, particularly in infants and travellers not accustomed to high-sulfate water. WHO has no health-based guideline but flags aesthetic concerns above 250 mg/L. High sulfate promotes sulfate-reducing bacteria that produce hydrogen sulfide (rotten egg odour) and accelerates iron and concrete pipe corrosion.
sulfate.detection: Laboratory analysis or specific ion test kits. Sulfate concentration is commonly included in comprehensive water quality panels. A sulphurous smell in water may suggest high sulfate or bacterial sulfate reduction. Annual laboratory testing recommended for private wells in areas with sulfate-bearing geology.

chloride.name: Chloride
chloride.description: Chloride ions affect water taste and indicate potential contamination from road salt, sewage, or seawater intrusion.
chloride.sources: Natural geological sources from salt deposits and marine sediments. Road deicing salt is the dominant anthropogenic source in cold climates, increasingly contaminating groundwater. Sewage and septic system effluent. Agricultural fertilisers. Seawater and brackish groundwater intrusion in coastal areas.
chloride.healthRisks: Chloride itself has no health-based guideline from WHO. Concentrations above 250 mg/L cause a salty taste and accelerate pipe corrosion. High chloride is an important indicator of sewage, road salt, or seawater contamination — all of which carry more significant health risks. Elevated chloride with elevated nitrates strongly suggests sewage or agricultural impact.
chloride.detection: Laboratory analysis or chloride-specific test kits and strips. Salty taste is detectable above approximately 250 mg/L. Chloride is routinely measured in comprehensive water quality tests. If chloride is rising over time in a well, investigate potential road salt migration or sewage contamination.

sodium.name: Sodium
sodium.description: Elevated sodium in drinking water is a concern for people on low-sodium diets and is often a sign of water softening or contamination.
sodium.sources: Ion exchange water softeners exchange calcium and magnesium for sodium, often significantly raising sodium concentration. Road salt runoff into groundwater. Seawater intrusion. Sewage and industrial wastewater. Natural dissolution from salt-bearing geological formations.
sodium.healthRisks: People on sodium-restricted diets for hypertension or heart and kidney disease may need to monitor drinking water sodium. WHO guideline is 200 mg/L. Water softened by ion exchange can reach 100–400 mg/L sodium depending on original hardness. Infants should not consume formula made with high-sodium water. RO and distillation are the only reliable methods to reduce sodium.
sodium.detection: Laboratory flame photometry or ICP-OES analysis. Sodium is not detectable by taste at typical drinking water concentrations. Ask your water softener installer about expected sodium output based on your water hardness. Include sodium in any comprehensive water quality test if you or household members are on sodium-restricted diets.

aluminum.name: Aluminum
aluminum.description: Residual aluminum from water treatment coagulation or natural geology can accumulate in water supply systems.
aluminum.sources: Aluminium sulphate (alum) is the most widely used coagulant in municipal water treatment; residual aluminium remains if treatment is not optimised. Natural dissolution from acidic soils and rocks, particularly in low-pH soft water. Industrial discharge from aluminium smelting and manufacturing. Acid rain accelerates aluminium leaching from soils into surface water.
aluminum.healthRisks: WHO guideline is 0.2 mg/L for treated water. Aluminium accumulates in bone, brain, and kidney tissue with long-term high exposure. The link between aluminium and Alzheimer's disease remains contested in the scientific literature. Dialysis dementia (dialysis encephalopathy) is a confirmed risk from aluminium-contaminated dialysis water. Infants may be more sensitive due to higher absorption rates.
aluminum.detection: ICP-OES or ICP-MS laboratory analysis. Standard water quality reports from municipal systems include aluminium monitoring. Optimised coagulation-filtration at the treatment plant is the main control measure. For private wells in areas with acidic soils, include aluminium in periodic water quality testing.

nickel.name: Nickel
nickel.description: Nickel leaches from plumbing fixtures and fittings and is a common trace contaminant in groundwater near industrial sites.
nickel.sources: Chrome-plated taps, fittings, and stainless steel components leach nickel, particularly in new installations and with corrosive water. Natural occurrence in ultramafic and mafic geological formations. Industrial discharge from electroplating, battery manufacturing, and mining. Nickel-containing alloys used in some water system components.
nickel.healthRisks: WHO guideline is 0.07 mg/L. Nickel is the most common cause of allergic contact dermatitis and can trigger dermatitis through drinking water in sensitised individuals. Animal studies show carcinogenicity via inhalation; evidence for drinking water carcinogenicity is less conclusive. Elevated nickel in groundwater is a common problem in areas of mafic geology.
nickel.detection: ICP-OES or ICP-MS laboratory analysis. First-flush sampling (first litre after overnight standing) shows worst-case leaching from plumbing. NSF 58 and NSF 42 certified filters have been tested against nickel. Flush cold water tap for 30 seconds before using for drinking to reduce first-flush nickel from fittings.

selenium.name: Selenium
selenium.description: Selenium is an essential trace element at low doses but toxic at elevated concentrations found in certain geological regions.
selenium.sources: Natural leaching from selenium-rich sedimentary rocks and shales — a significant problem in parts of South and North America, China, and India. Agricultural irrigation drainage concentrates selenium from seleniferous soils. Coal combustion and fly ash disposal. Phosphate mining operations.
selenium.healthRisks: WHO guideline is 0.04 mg/L. Selenium is an essential micronutrient at trace levels (dietary requirement ~55 µg/day) but toxic at elevated concentrations. Selenosis from chronic high intake causes hair loss, nail brittleness, neurological damage, and in severe cases paralysis. Cardiovascular effects observed at 0.1–0.3 mg/L. The margin between deficiency and toxicity is narrow.
selenium.detection: ICP-MS or hydride generation atomic absorption spectrometry. Standard laboratory water quality panels often omit selenium — request it specifically if in a high-selenium geological area. US EPA and WHO include selenium in regulatory monitoring of public water supplies. Private wells in selenium-affected agricultural areas should be tested annually.

barium.name: Barium
barium.description: Barium is a naturally occurring metal in some groundwater with cardiovascular effects at elevated concentrations.
barium.sources: Natural dissolution from barite (BaSO₄) and witherite (BaCO₃) mineral deposits in sedimentary rock formations. Drilling fluids from oil and gas operations. Industrial discharge from chemical manufacturing and coal combustion. Areas of evaporite geology in arid regions.
barium.healthRisks: WHO guideline is 0.7 mg/L. Barium causes cardiovascular effects: elevated blood pressure, cardiac arrhythmia, and muscle weakness at concentrations above the guideline. Barium sulphate is insoluble and inert (used medically as an X-ray contrast agent), but soluble barium salts are toxic. Long-term low-level exposure studies show hypertensive effects.
barium.detection: ICP-OES or ICP-MS laboratory analysis. Barium is not detectable by taste or odour. Include in comprehensive water quality testing for private wells in areas with barite geology or near oil and gas operations. Ion exchange softeners effectively remove barium — EPA confirms this as a best available technology.

asbestos.name: Asbestos Fibres
asbestos.description: Microscopic asbestos fibres from deteriorating asbestos cement water mains contaminate drinking water in older infrastructure.
asbestos.sources: Asbestos cement (AC) water mains were widely installed from the 1930s through 1980s and remain in service in many cities. As pipes age, corrode, or are disturbed during construction, chrysotile and amphibole asbestos fibres are released into the water. Countries with extensive legacy AC infrastructure include the UK, Canada, Australia, and much of Eastern Europe.
asbestos.healthRisks: The carcinogenicity of ingested asbestos fibres is less certain than for inhaled fibres, but WHO classifies asbestos as a known human carcinogen and advises precautionary reduction. Some studies link high gastrointestinal asbestos fibre levels to colon and stomach cancers. Children may absorb ingested fibres more readily than adults. The risk scales with fibre concentration and duration of exposure.
asbestos.detection: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) or phase contrast microscopy is required — asbestos fibres are invisible to the naked eye and below detection of standard turbidity measurements. Contact your water utility to determine if your area has AC mains; utilities in many countries are required to map and report their pipe material inventory. Point-of-use hollow fibre or reverse osmosis filters effectively remove fibres.

helminths.name: Helminths (Intestinal Parasitic Worms)
helminths.description: Parasitic worm eggs and larvae transmitted through contaminated water — a major cause of intestinal disease in tropical and subtropical regions. Unlike bacteria and protozoa, helminths are not killed by standard chlorine disinfection.
helminths.sources: Human and animal faeces contaminating water sources. Helminth eggs are shed in stool and can survive in soil and water for months to years. Open defecation near water bodies, flooding that disperses faecal material, and use of untreated human waste as fertiliser are the primary transmission pathways. Common water-transmitted species include Ascaris lumbricoides (roundworm), Trichuris trichiura (whipworm), hookworms, and Schistosoma (sub-Saharan Africa and parts of Asia).
helminths.healthRisks: Heavy intestinal worm burdens cause malnutrition, stunted growth, anaemia, and cognitive impairment — particularly damaging in children under five. Schistosomiasis (bilharzia) causes chronic damage to the liver, bladder, and intestines and affects over 240 million people worldwide. Helminth infections are among the most prevalent infectious diseases globally, though individual infections often produce few acute symptoms for months to years.
helminths.detection: Eggs are visible under microscopy in stool samples — laboratory analysis is the standard detection method. Standard water quality tests (coliform testing, turbidity) do not detect helminth eggs. Critical: chlorination does not kill helminth eggs. Physical removal through filtration (ceramic, hollow fibre, biosand) or heat treatment (boiling, distillation) is required. This is a critical difference from bacterial or viral contamination.

legionella.name: Legionella (Legionnaires' Disease)
legionella.description: A bacterium that grows in warm building water systems and causes severe pneumonia — Legionnaires' disease — transmitted by inhaling contaminated aerosols, not by drinking water.
legionella.sources: Legionella bacteria naturally occur in freshwater at low levels. They proliferate to dangerous concentrations in building water systems where warm water stagnates: hot water tanks, cooling towers, decorative fountains, showerheads, and pipe dead-ends. Water temperatures of 25–45°C support rapid growth; above 60°C, Legionella is killed within seconds. Legionella is not typically a source water concern — it is predominantly a building water management problem.
legionella.healthRisks: Legionnaires' disease is a severe pneumonia with approximately 10% fatality in community cases and up to 25–30% in hospitalised patients. CDC data identifies Legionella as the leading cause of reported waterborne disease deaths in the United States (approximately 6,000–18,000 cases annually). Elderly people, smokers, and immunocompromised individuals face the highest risk.
legionella.detection: Clinical diagnosis uses urine antigen testing or sputum culture. Building water system testing requires water sampling and culture analysis (ISO 11731) — results take 10–14 days. Critical: standard household drinking water treatment (filtration, chlorination, UV) can address Legionella in source water but does not prevent re-growth in building plumbing systems. Hot water must be stored above 60°C and delivered above 50°C; cold water must be kept below 20°C. Building water safety plans are required for complex water systems (hospitals, hotels, care homes).

ammonia.name: Ammonia / Ammonium
ammonia.description: A dissolved nitrogen compound that signals sewage or organic matter contamination and interferes with chlorine disinfection by reacting to form the weaker disinfectant chloramine.
ammonia.sources: Decomposition of organic nitrogen compounds — faecal matter, animal waste, and decaying vegetation. Sewage and industrial wastewater discharge. Agricultural runoff from ammonium-based fertilisers. Natural microbial activity in groundwater. Elevated ammonia in groundwater is often a reliable indicator of recent faecal contamination or long-term anaerobic conditions.
ammonia.healthRisks: WHO aesthetic guideline: 1.5 mg/L. At typical drinking water concentrations, ammonia itself is not acutely toxic to humans. Its primary importance is as a contamination indicator and as a factor that severely impairs chlorine disinfection. When chlorine is added to ammonia-containing water, it preferentially reacts to form chloramines instead of the more effective free chlorine — substantially reducing disinfection performance against viruses and bacteria. Detectable ammonia in finished treated water indicates inadequate treatment.
ammonia.detection: Colourimetric test kits (Nessler or salicylate methods) are widely available and reliable. Standard water quality panels include ammonia measurement. Any detectable ammonia in finished treated water should trigger investigation of source contamination or treatment failure. Presence in groundwater near latrines or agricultural land indicates a potential faecal contamination pathway.

boron.name: Boron
boron.description: A naturally occurring element elevated in geothermal groundwater, seawater-influenced aquifers, and regions with boron mining — a reproductive toxin at elevated concentrations.
boron.sources: Natural geothermal and volcanic geological formations leach boron into groundwater — a significant problem in Turkey, Chile, Argentina, parts of the US Southwest, and Mediterranean coastal regions. Seawater contains approximately 5 mg/L boron; seawater intrusion raises concentrations in coastal aquifers. Industrial sources include glass and ceramic manufacturing, boron ore mining, and some fertiliser formulations.
boron.healthRisks: WHO provisional guideline: 2.4 mg/L. Boron is an essential trace element at low dietary doses but toxic at higher exposures. The primary concern is reproductive toxicity: animal studies show decreased sperm motility, testicular damage, and foetal abnormalities at doses relevant to high-boron drinking water. Human studies in high-boron communities in Turkey showed reduced birth rates at water concentrations above 4 mg/L. Standard filtration and activated carbon do not remove boron.
boron.detection: ICP-OES or ICP-MS laboratory analysis. Colourimetric test kits (curcumin or carmine methods) are available but less sensitive. Geological maps and regional groundwater surveys provide first-pass risk assessment. Testing is recommended in any area with geothermal activity, saline aquifer influence, or historical boron mining. Only reverse osmosis and specialised selective ion exchange resins effectively remove boron.

cyanide.name: Free Cyanide
cyanide.description: A highly toxic industrial chemical that contaminates water near gold and silver mining operations through cyanide heap-leach processes and tailings discharge.
cyanide.sources: Artisanal and industrial gold mining using cyanide heap leaching is the primary source in affected regions. Industrial chemical manufacture, electroplating, and metal processing discharge cyanide-bearing wastewater. Natural sources (cassava, bitter almonds) release hydrogen cyanide but represent negligible drinking water contamination compared to industrial sources. Cyanide is rapidly degraded by sunlight and microbial activity in aerobic conditions but can persist in closed or shaded systems.
cyanide.healthRisks: WHO guideline: 0.07 mg/L as free cyanide. Cyanide inhibits cytochrome c oxidase, blocking cellular respiration — even small doses can cause rapid tissue asphyxia. Acute exposure above 1 mg/L causes headache, dizziness, and vomiting; higher doses can cause rapid loss of consciousness and death. At guideline-relevant concentrations, chronic exposure has been associated with neurological and thyroid effects. Communities near active mining operations face the highest risk of drinking water cyanide contamination.
cyanide.detection: Colourimetric field kits (pyridine-barbituric acid or pyridine-pyrazolone methods) provide screening. Laboratory analysis by ion-selective electrode or spectrophotometry (US EPA Method 335.4) is required for regulatory compliance. Free cyanide degrades rapidly on exposure to sunlight and air — samples must be preserved with sodium hydroxide and analysed promptly. Test near mining sites and following tailings pond incidents or spills.

boiling.name: Boiling
boiling.description: The most universally accessible disinfection method — kills bacteria, viruses, and protozoa without any equipment.
boiling.howItWorks: Sustained boiling at 100 °C kills all biological pathogens by denaturing proteins and disrupting cell membranes and nucleic acids. One minute of rolling boil is sufficient at sea level; three minutes above 2000 m altitude where water boils at lower temperature. Bring water to a full rolling boil — vigorous bubbling, not a simmer.
boiling.limitations: Does not remove chemical contaminants, heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, PFAS, or dissolved inorganic compounds — and may concentrate them slightly as water evaporates. Requires a reliable fuel source, adding cost and time. Smoke from solid fuel combustion causes serious indoor air pollution in poorly ventilated settings. Re-contamination occurs if water is stored in dirty containers after boiling. Critical: when cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) are present in the water, boiling kills the cells but causes them to lyse and release dissolved cyanotoxins — boiling does not make bloom-affected water safe to drink. Activated carbon adsorption or reverse osmosis is required to remove dissolved cyanotoxins.
boiling.typicalUse: Emergency disinfection in any setting. Daily household use where fuel is accessible and consistent. Backup method when other treatment systems fail or are unavailable.

biosand.name: Biosand Filter
biosand.description: A DIY sand filter that develops a biological layer reducing bacteria, protozoa, and turbidity — with limited virus removal.
biosand.howItWorks: Water percolates slowly through layers of fine sand and gravel. Over 2–3 weeks, a biological layer (Schmutzdecke) of predatory microorganisms forms on the sand surface and consumes incoming pathogens. Physical straining simultaneously removes turbidity and large particles. Flow rate must remain low (0.1–0.4 m/h) to allow adequate contact time with the biological layer.
biosand.limitations: Does not reliably remove viruses — virus removal is typically 0–1 log reduction, which is insufficient for virus-contaminated water. Requires 2–3 weeks to establish the biological layer before effective treatment begins. Slow flow rate (0.3–0.6 L/min). The biolayer dies if the filter dries out — requires daily use. No effect on dissolved chemicals, heavy metals, or fluoride.
biosand.typicalUse: Rural and peri-urban communities with consistently turbid surface water where the primary concern is bacterial and protozoan contamination and local construction materials are available. Should be combined with chlorination or boiling when viral contamination is suspected.
biosand.contextNote: The biological layer (schmutzdecke) requires 2-4 weeks to establish. Consistent daily use is essential — the filter must not dry out.

ceramic_filtration.name: Ceramic Filtration
ceramic_filtration.description: Porous ceramic pots physically remove bacteria, protozoa, and suspended solids by straining them through sub-micron pores.
ceramic_filtration.howItWorks: Micropores in fired clay (0.1–1 µm in diameter) physically block bacteria and protozoa. Many pots are impregnated with colloidal silver for additional bactericidal effect at the filter surface. Water flows by gravity through the ceramic element into a lower storage vessel, typically producing 1–3 litres per hour.
ceramic_filtration.limitations: Does not remove viruses (20–300 nm — smaller than ceramic pore size). Slow throughput (1–3 L/hr). Fragile — any crack or chip eliminates filtration effectiveness. Requires regular gentle cleaning to prevent clogging. No effect on dissolved chemicals, nitrates, fluoride, or heavy metals. Storage vessel must be kept clean and covered.
ceramic_filtration.typicalUse: Household point-of-use treatment where the primary concern is bacterial contamination and turbidity. Widely deployed in sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and Central America. Must be combined with a viral disinfection step when viral contamination is a concern.

activated_carbon.name: Activated Carbon
activated_carbon.description: Adsorbs chlorine, chlorination by-products, many pesticides, and organic chemicals through an enormous internal surface area.
activated_carbon.howItWorks: Highly porous activated carbon provides an enormous internal surface area (700–1500 m²/g) that adsorbs organic molecules, free chlorine, trihalomethanes, pesticides, herbicides, and volatile organic compounds. Water passes through a solid carbon block or granular activated carbon (GAC) bed. NSF/ANSI 53-certified carbon blocks can also reduce lead specifically through adsorption when tested and certified for this purpose — standard carbon does not reliably remove lead.
activated_carbon.limitations: Does not kill or remove biological pathogens — must be combined with a disinfection method. Carbon bed becomes saturated and must be replaced every 3–6 months (or per manufacturer specification) — an exhausted carbon filter provides no adsorption and may release accumulated contaminants. Effectiveness depends on sufficient contact time with the carbon bed; high flow rates reduce removal efficiency. No effect on nitrates, fluoride, dissolved inorganic salts, or heavy metals (except NSF 53-certified lead reduction). Does not remove pharmaceuticals, hormones, or PFAS at concentrations of health concern.
activated_carbon.typicalUse: Post-disinfection polishing to improve taste, odour, and appearance of chlorinated tap water. Chemical pre-treatment stage before UV or RO to protect membranes from chlorine damage. Reduction of pesticide and VOC contamination from agricultural and industrial groundwater.
activated_carbon.contextNote: Performance depends on remaining cartridge capacity. Part of most multi-stage systems; does not replace disinfection.

uv_disinfection.name: UV Disinfection
uv_disinfection.description: Ultraviolet light inactivates bacteria, viruses, and protozoa — including Giardia and Cryptosporidium — when water is pre-filtered and the system operates at a certified UV dose.
uv_disinfection.howItWorks: UV-C light at 254 nm penetrates microbial cells and causes thymine dimer formation in DNA, preventing reproduction. A UV lamp in a flow-through chamber irradiates water passing at a controlled flow rate. The required dose for safe disinfection is typically 40 mJ/cm². Bacteria and most viruses are inactivated at this standard dose. Giardia cysts require 10 mJ/cm²; Cryptosporidium oocysts require 10–40 mJ/cm² for adequate inactivation — requiring a validated system, not a generic lamp.
uv_disinfection.limitations: No effect on turbid water — suspended particles physically shield microbes from UV radiation. Pre-filtration to below 1 NTU is required before UV treatment. Protozoan inactivation (including Cryptosporidium) requires a system delivering at least 40 mJ/cm² — use only Class A certified systems (NSF/ANSI 55) for reliable protozoan control. Provides no residual disinfection after treatment — water can be re-contaminated in storage. Requires stable electricity and annual lamp replacement. Does not remove any chemical contaminants, heavy metals, nitrates, or dissolved solids. When cyanobacteria cells are present, UV inactivates cells but may cause lysis, releasing dissolved cyanotoxins — activated carbon or RO is required to remove those toxins.
uv_disinfection.typicalUse: Final disinfection stage for pre-filtered water in household under-sink systems, community water kiosks, and field treatment trains. Particularly valuable as a chlorine-free disinfection option where taste and DBP concerns are important.
uv_disinfection.contextNote: Requires low turbidity (< 1 NTU). Typically installed last, after sediment and carbon filtration.

reverse_osmosis.name: Reverse Osmosis
reverse_osmosis.description: Removes the majority of dissolved inorganic contaminants — heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, and salinity — alongside biological and particulate contamination.
