reverse_osmosis.howItWorks: Pressurised water is forced through a semi-permeable membrane with approximately 0.0001 µm pores. The membrane rejects dissolved ions, heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, microorganisms, and most organic molecules. Rejection efficiency ranges from 85–99% depending on the contaminant and system design. Rejected contaminants are flushed away as brine; purified water passes through to a storage tank. Post-treatment remineralisation is recommended for long-term drinking use as RO also removes beneficial calcium and magnesium.
reverse_osmosis.limitations: Requires staged pre-filtration: a sediment filter first (to remove particles that would foul the carbon), followed by an activated carbon filter (to remove chlorine that would destroy the membrane). Arsenic removal depends on oxidation state: As(V) is rejected at 90%+ by certified systems; As(III) is poorly rejected — oxidation pre-treatment (e.g. aeration or chlorination) is required for reliable arsenic removal. Produces 3–4 litres of brine waste per litre purified in standard household units; high-efficiency systems can approach 1:1 recovery. Requires inlet pressure of 4–8 bar — a booster pump is usually necessary. Does not remove dissolved gases (radon, hydrogen sulphide, carbon dioxide, some volatile chlorination by-products). Membranes require replacement every 2–3 years; pre-filters every 6–12 months. Membrane integrity cannot be verified without pressure-decay testing. In settings with high biological contamination risk, a downstream UV or chlorination step is advisable.
reverse_osmosis.typicalUse: Households facing dissolved inorganic contamination that other methods cannot address — high salinity, nitrates, fluoride, arsenic, lead, or heavy metals — with access to reliable electricity and technical maintenance capability.
reverse_osmosis.contextNote: Requires pre-filtration (sediment + activated carbon) to protect the membrane. Often the final stage in a multi-step system.

chlorination.name: Chlorination
chlorination.description: Adding chlorine tablets or solution kills bacteria and viruses and provides residual protection during water storage.
chlorination.howItWorks: Chlorine reacts with water to form hypochlorous acid (HOCl), which penetrates microbial cell walls and disrupts enzymes, killing bacteria and viruses within 30 minutes at adequate concentrations. A maintained residual of 0.2–0.5 mg/L free chlorine provides protection against re-contamination during storage. Efficacy depends on contact time, pH, temperature, and water clarity.
chlorination.limitations: Requires adequate contact time — a minimum of 30 minutes at standard dosage — before water reaches the consumer. Cryptosporidium is essentially impervious to practical chlorine doses; Giardia requires very high CT values rarely achievable in field conditions. Physical filtration is required to address protozoan contamination. Reacts with natural organic matter to form trihalomethanes (THMs) and haloacetic acids (HAAs), classified as probable human carcinogens; risk increases with organic-rich source water. Does not remove pharmaceuticals, hormones, heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, PFAS, or turbidity. Taste and odour can be unpleasant at effective doses.
chlorination.typicalUse: Point-of-use disinfection using sodium dichloroisocyanurate (NaDCC) tablets or household bleach. Particularly valuable in emergencies and displacement settings where portability, cost, and residual protection are critical. Always use pre-settled or pre-filtered water to ensure effective contact.
chlorination.contextNote: Requires sufficient contact time before the water reaches the consumer. Often followed by activated carbon to remove taste and disinfection by-products.

hollow_fiber.name: Hollow Fiber Ultrafiltration
hollow_fiber.description: A membrane technology that physically removes bacteria, protozoa, and particles using bundles of hollow polymer fibres with sub-micron pores.
hollow_fiber.howItWorks: Water is driven through bundles of hollow fibres made from polyethersulfone or PVDF polymer. Pore sizes of 0.01–0.1 µm physically block bacteria, protozoa, cyanobacteria, and particles larger than the pore size. The membrane acts as a physical barrier — no chemicals are required. Gravity-operated hollow fibre units are available for off-grid settings. Back-flushing (reversing flow) periodically clears accumulated material from the membrane surface.
hollow_fiber.limitations: Does not remove dissolved chemicals, heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, cyanotoxins, or viruses (most UF membranes do not provide reliable virus removal — only nanofiltration and RO do). Requires periodic back-flushing and periodic chemical cleaning. Membranes must be replaced every 2–5 years. Performance degrades with highly turbid or chemically complex water.
hollow_fiber.typicalUse: Point-of-use household units and community water kiosks where biological contamination is the primary concern. Emergency response for rapid deployment. Pre-treatment stage before RO or UV in multi-stage systems.
hollow_fiber.contextNote: Does not remove dissolved chemicals or heavy metals. Typically combined with activated carbon for comprehensive treatment.

distillation.name: Distillation
distillation.description: Boils water and recondenses the steam, leaving dissolved salts, metals, and most contaminants behind in the residue.
distillation.howItWorks: Water is heated to boiling; steam rises, leaving dissolved salts, metals, and non-volatile contaminants in the boiling chamber. Steam condenses back to liquid in a separate cooling chamber, producing purified distillate. Electrical countertop distillers process approximately 3–5 litres per 4–6 hour cycle. Biological pathogens are killed during the boiling process.
distillation.limitations: Does not remove volatile organic compounds (VOCs) — these have boiling points near or below water and carry over into the distillate. A carbon post-filter is recommended when VOC contamination is a concern. Some volatile pesticides and chlorine may also carry over. Energy-intensive (approximately 0.7–1 kWh per litre). Slow throughput. Removes beneficial minerals including calcium and magnesium — remineralisation recommended for long-term use. Provides no residual disinfection protection.
distillation.typicalUse: Households facing dissolved inorganic contamination (arsenic, fluoride, nitrates, heavy metals, high salinity) where reverse osmosis is not available or practical. Emergency water production from diverse or unknown sources.
distillation.contextNote: VOC carry-over risk; a carbon post-filter is recommended for complete treatment.

ion_exchange.name: Ion Exchange
ion_exchange.description: Replaces unwanted dissolved ions in water with less harmful ones using a bed of charged resin — targeting specific chemical contaminants.
ion_exchange.howItWorks: Ion exchange resin beads in a pressure vessel selectively capture target ions from water, releasing a pre-loaded counter-ion in their place. Cation exchange resins (sodium or hydrogen form) capture positively charged ions such as lead, cadmium, calcium, and magnesium. Anion exchange resins (chloride or hydroxide form) capture negatively charged ions including nitrates, fluoride, arsenic (V), and chromate. Resin requires periodic regeneration with a concentrated salt solution to restore capacity.
ion_exchange.limitations: Does not remove biological pathogens, turbidity, pharmaceuticals, hormones, or non-ionic organic chemicals including pesticides and VOCs — residential ion exchange systems are not designed for these contaminants. Resin exhaustion must be monitored — exhausted resin provides no treatment. Regeneration produces a concentrated brine waste stream requiring proper disposal. Specific resin formulations are required for different target contaminants. Arsenic removal efficiency depends on speciation — arsenate (As V) is well removed, arsenite (As III) is not.
ion_exchange.typicalUse: Nitrate removal from drinking water in agricultural regions. Arsenic removal in endemic geological areas using iron oxide or alumina anion exchange media. Hardness removal in commercial water softeners. High-purity water production using mixed-bed deionisation for laboratory and industrial applications.

water_softening.name: Water Softening
water_softening.description: A cation exchange process that replaces calcium and magnesium responsible for hardness and scale with sodium, protecting plumbing and appliances.
water_softening.howItWorks: A sodium-form strong-acid cation exchange resin replaces calcium (Ca²⁺) and magnesium (Mg²⁺) ions with sodium (Na⁺) ions, eliminating scale-forming potential. Hardness is measured before and after to verify performance. The resin is regenerated periodically by flushing with concentrated sodium chloride (brine) solution, which displaces accumulated calcium and magnesium and restores sodium form.
water_softening.limitations: Does not remove biological pathogens, nitrates, fluoride, heavy metals, or most dissolved chemicals. Increases the sodium content of water — medically significant for individuals on sodium-restricted diets; typically adds 20–50 mg/L additional sodium per 100 mg/L CaCO₃ of hardness removed. Does not reduce total dissolved solids. Brine waste requires proper disposal and may be restricted by local regulations. Not recommended as the sole treatment for drinking water.
water_softening.typicalUse: Household and commercial water treatment in hard water areas (above 200 mg/L CaCO₃) to prevent scale in boilers, water heaters, washing machines, and dishwashers. Often installed with a dedicated bypass line providing untreated hard water for drinking, as softened water's elevated sodium content makes it unsuitable for direct consumption by some groups.

sediment_filtration.name: Sediment / Mechanical Filtration
sediment_filtration.description: A physical pre-treatment step that removes suspended particles and sediment using a porous filter cartridge or media bed.
sediment_filtration.howItWorks: Water passes through a filter medium — polypropylene wound or melt-blown cartridge, ceramic, or a multimedia sand and gravel bed — that physically traps particles above a rated size (typically 1–50 µm). Finer-rated filters capture smaller particles but require more frequent replacement or backwashing. Sediment filters are always the first stage in multi-stage treatment systems.
sediment_filtration.limitations: Does not remove dissolved chemical contaminants, biological pathogens, or chemical pollutants. Clogged or exhausted filters must be replaced or backwashed regularly — a blocked filter creates high pressure differential and can rupture, and it provides no filtration if bypassed by cracks. Fine bacteria and viruses pass through all standard sediment filter ratings.
sediment_filtration.typicalUse: First pre-treatment stage in multi-stage systems (RO, UV, activated carbon) to protect downstream components from physical damage and premature fouling. Essential wherever source water contains significant suspended solids from turbid wells, rivers, or surface water.

solar_disinfection.name: Solar Disinfection (SODIS)
solar_disinfection.description: A free, zero-equipment disinfection method recommended by WHO and UNICEF — using sunlight to inactivate pathogens in water stored in transparent PET plastic bottles.
solar_disinfection.howItWorks: Clear 1.5–2 litre PET plastic bottles are filled with pre-settled or pre-filtered water (turbidity below 30 NTU), shaken to ensure oxygen saturation, and placed horizontally on a reflective surface in direct sunlight for a minimum of 6 hours under clear-sky conditions or 2 consecutive days under overcast conditions. UV-A radiation (315–400 nm) and solar thermal heating both contribute to pathogen inactivation. Above 50°C combined with UV-A exposure (the SODIS synergy effect), inactivation is substantially accelerated. Bottles must be clean, undamaged, and clear — green, tinted, or heavily scratched bottles significantly reduce UV penetration.
solar_disinfection.limitations: Does not remove chemical contaminants, heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, or other dissolved substances — SODIS is purely a disinfection method. Requires turbidity below 30 NTU — pre-filtering through clean cloth is necessary for turbid water; effectiveness drops sharply above this threshold. Does not work in opaque, green, or coloured bottles — clear PET only. Less effective in cool climates with frequent cloud cover. Provides no residual protection — re-contamination occurs if treated water is transferred to a dirty container.
solar_disinfection.typicalUse: Primary household point-of-use disinfection where fuel for boiling, electricity for UV systems, or chlorine tablets are unavailable. Primary recommendation by WHO and UNICEF for off-grid households in tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, South Asia, and Latin America. Compatible with cloth or ceramic pre-filtration for turbid source water. Works with any water source once turbidity is below 30 NTU.

coagulation_flocculation.name: Coagulation, Flocculation & Sedimentation
coagulation_flocculation.description: A pre-treatment process that removes suspended particles, colour, and turbidity by causing fine particles to aggregate and settle — the essential first step before filtration and disinfection of turbid surface water.
coagulation_flocculation.howItWorks: A coagulant chemical — typically aluminium sulphate (alum) or ferric sulphate — is added to raw water and mixed rapidly for 1–2 minutes. The coagulant neutralises the negative surface charges keeping fine particles suspended, causing them to clump together. Slow gentle mixing for 15–30 minutes then allows aggregated particles to grow into visible flocs. Flocs are allowed to settle by gravity over 30–60 minutes in a settling vessel. The clarified water above the settled floc is then carefully decanted for filtration and disinfection. A standard alum dose is 5–50 mg/L depending on turbidity — a simple jar test can identify the optimal dose for a specific water source.
coagulation_flocculation.limitations: Does not remove dissolved chemicals, heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, viruses, or dissolved cyanotoxins (though it physically removes cyanobacteria cells). Residual aluminium may remain if dose is not well controlled — overdosing increases, rather than decreases, residual aluminium. Does not reliably inactivate bacteria or viruses on its own — downstream disinfection is always required. Requires correct dosing, mixing speed, and settling time. Produces a sludge that must be properly disposed of.
coagulation_flocculation.typicalUse: Essential pre-treatment for highly turbid surface water (rivers, ponds, lakes) before biosand filtration, ceramic filtration, UV disinfection, or chlorination. Widely used in community water treatment in rural settings using locally available alum. First step in all conventional municipal water treatment plants worldwide. Reduces filter clogging and dramatically increases the effectiveness of downstream disinfection by removing the particles that shield and harbour pathogens.

aeration.name: Aeration
aeration.description: Exposing water to air to remove dissolved gases (hydrogen sulfide, radon, CO₂) and oxidise dissolved iron and manganese into filterable precipitates — the primary treatment approach for many private well water problems.
aeration.howItWorks: Dissolved gases are stripped from water as it is exposed to air over a large surface area — achieved by spraying, cascading over trays, or agitating in an open vessel. Simultaneously, dissolved oxygen in air oxidises ferrous iron (Fe²⁺, colourless) to ferric hydroxide (Fe(OH)₃, rust-coloured floc) and manganese (Mn²⁺) to manganese dioxide (MnO₂, black precipitate). These insoluble precipitates are then removed by a downstream sediment filter. Simple cascade aeration requires no electricity or chemicals — water falls through multiple trays or over a series of steps.
aeration.limitations: Does not remove bacteria, viruses, protozoa, dissolved salts, most heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, or most chemical contaminants. Aeration of iron or manganese-bearing water produces visible precipitates that must be removed by a downstream sediment filter — aeration alone is insufficient. Removal of CO₂ raises pH, which may help or hinder downstream treatment depending on the system. In heavily polluted environments, open aeration may introduce airborne contaminants into water.
aeration.typicalUse: Pre-treatment for well water with elevated iron, manganese, or hydrogen sulfide odour. Radon removal from well water in granite-rich geological regions. Taste and odour improvement for groundwater with elevated CO₂ or volatile organic compounds. Always followed by a sediment filter to capture the iron and manganese precipitates produced. Simple and effective first step in private well treatment systems with no running costs.

sediment_5um.name: Sediment Filter 5µm
sediment_5um.description: Removes sand, rust, and suspended particles down to 5 microns.

activated_carbon_block.name: Activated Carbon Block
activated_carbon_block.description: Removes chlorine, taste, odour, pesticides, and organic chemicals.

ceramic_candle.name: Ceramic Candle Filter
ceramic_candle.description: A porous ceramic element that blocks bacteria and protozoa by physical straining.

biosand_diy.name: DIY Biosand Filter
biosand_diy.description: A locally built sand filter that reduces bacteria, protozoa, and turbidity.

uv_lamp_6w.name: UV Lamp 6W
uv_lamp_6w.description: A 6-watt UV-C lamp for disinfecting up to 6 litres per minute.

ro_membrane_75gpd.name: RO Membrane 75 GPD
ro_membrane_75gpd.description: Reverse osmosis membrane removing dissolved salts, heavy metals, and most contaminants.

slow_sand_diy.name: DIY Slow Sand Filter
slow_sand_diy.description: A large-scale gravity filter using fine sand to reduce pathogens and turbidity.

chlorine_tablet.name: Chlorine Tablets (NaDCC)
chlorine_tablet.description: Sodium dichloroisocyanurate tablets for point-of-use bacterial and viral disinfection.

booster_pump.name: Booster Pump
booster_pump.description: Increases inlet water pressure to meet the requirements of high-pressure filters such as reverse osmosis.

water_softener.name: Water Softener
water_softener.description: Ion exchange unit that reduces water hardness (calcium and magnesium). Requires periodic salt regeneration. Not a reliable solution for significant iron or manganese — use dedicated iron/manganese treatment when those contaminants are present.

iron_manganese_filter.name: Iron & Manganese Filter
iron_manganese_filter.description: Oxidising media filter that removes dissolved iron and manganese from well or borehole water.

oxidation_filter.name: Oxidation Filter (H2S / Iron)
oxidation_filter.description: Removes hydrogen sulfide, iron, and manganese via oxidation and media filtration. Addresses rotten-egg odour.

basics.pageDescription: Foundational knowledge about water treatment: why multiple stages matter, how to evaluate technologies, and what common misconceptions to avoid.

multiStage.title: Multi-Stage Treatment
multiStage.intro: Most water sources contain a mix of contaminants that no single technology can address completely. Effective water treatment nearly always involves multiple stages working in sequence — each handling what the previous stage cannot.
multiStage.whySingleTitle: Why a Single Method Rarely Suffices
multiStage.whySingleBody: Individual treatment methods are designed to target specific contaminant classes. UV disinfection kills pathogens but leaves dissolved chemicals untouched. Activated carbon removes organics and chlorine but not heavy metals or nitrates. Reverse osmosis removes almost everything but requires a functioning membrane that chlorinated water will destroy without carbon pre-treatment. No single method provides complete protection against all contaminant classes simultaneously.
multiStage.trainConceptBody: A treatment train is a sequence of methods where each stage performs a specific function and may also protect downstream stages. The order matters: coarse filtration typically comes first to reduce turbidity, followed by finer filtration, followed by disinfection. Getting the order wrong wastes consumables and can allow failures the system was designed to prevent.

examples.name: Emergency field treatment
examples.note: For rivers, lakes, or turbid groundwater with no infrastructure. Coagulation settles suspended particles; ceramic removes pathogens and residual sediment; chlorination inactivates remaining bacteria and viruses.

examples.name: Urban tap water improvement
examples.note: For municipal tap water with chlorine, taste, and pathogen concerns. Sediment removes particles; carbon removes chlorine and organic compounds; UV provides final disinfection without adding chemicals.

examples.name: Well water with hardness
examples.chain: Sediment filter + water softener + activated carbon + UV
examples.note: For hard groundwater with biological risk. Sediment first protects the softener resin; softening removes calcium and magnesium; carbon polishes taste; UV disinfects.

multiStage.ctaText: Not sure which system fits your situation?

surfaceWater.title: Surface Water Risks
surfaceWater.intro: Rivers, lakes, reservoirs, and ponds present a broader and more variable range of contamination risks than groundwater. Their open surfaces and connections to catchment areas make them susceptible to biological, agricultural, and industrial inputs.
surfaceWater.biologyBody: Surface water receiving runoff from land with animal waste, human sewage, or decaying organic matter can carry bacteria (E. coli, Salmonella), viruses (hepatitis A, norovirus), protozoa (Cryptosporidium, Giardia), and helminths. Contamination levels fluctuate with rainfall, season, and upstream activity. Water that appears safe may become dangerous within hours after heavy rain.
surfaceWater.agricultureBody: Agricultural land adjacent to watercourses releases nitrates and phosphates from fertilisers, and pesticide and herbicide residues from crop treatment. These inputs peak immediately after application and during rainfall. Nitrate contamination of surface water used directly is a particular concern for infant feeding.
surfaceWater.industrialBody: Industrial activity upstream can introduce heavy metals (lead, cadmium, mercury, chromium), solvents, petroleum products, and PFAS into surface water. These inputs may be continuous or episodic. Unlike biological contamination, chemical contamination cannot be reversed by disinfection alone.
surfaceWater.clarityTitle: Visual Clarity Is Not a Safety Indicator
surfaceWater.clarityBody: Visual clarity is not a reliable indicator of microbiological or chemical safety. Pathogen concentrations sufficient to cause illness are invisible to the naked eye. Dissolved chemical contaminants produce no visible signal at any concentration of health relevance. A river running clear may carry more Cryptosporidium oocysts than when visibly turbid.
surfaceWater.minimumTreatmentBody: Surface water intended for drinking should receive at minimum: coagulation and settling or pre-filtration to reduce turbidity; fine filtration to remove pathogens (ceramic, hollow fibre, or RO); and disinfection (UV or chlorination) as a final safety barrier. A single stage such as boiling alone or chlorination alone is insufficient for untreated surface water.

waterTesting.title: Water Testing and Laboratory Analysis
waterTesting.intro: Choosing a treatment system without first testing the water is equivalent to prescribing medicine without a diagnosis. Testing establishes the baseline contamination profile, guides technology selection, and verifies that a treatment system is performing as expected.
whenItems: Before installing a treatment system — to identify which contaminants are present and at what concentrations.
After installing a new system — to verify the system achieves the required reductions.
Periodically during operation — at least annually for private wells; more frequently if source conditions change.
After any known contamination event: flooding, agricultural activity, or construction near the water source.
If taste, colour, or odour changes unexpectedly.
typesItems: Basic potability panel: total coliform, E. coli, nitrates, pH, turbidity. Suitable for initial biological and chemical screening.
Heavy metals panel: lead, arsenic, cadmium, mercury, chromium, copper. Required for well water in areas with mining, industrial activity, or old plumbing.
Full chemical analysis: covers ions, minerals, and synthetic chemicals including PFAS and pesticides. Required when source is surface water or contamination history is unknown.
waterTesting.interpretBody: WHO guideline values represent concentrations below which no significant health risk is expected over a lifetime of consumption. A result 'below detection limit' does not mean a contaminant is absent — it means the concentration is below what the instrument can measure. Always compare results against the applicable national standard and the WHO guideline.
waterTesting.kitsVsLabTitle: Home Test Kits vs. Accredited Laboratory
waterTesting.kitsVsLabBody: Home test kits are suitable for monitoring specific parameters (nitrates, chlorine, pH) once a system is established. They are not reliable for establishing a baseline contamination profile or detecting pathogens, heavy metals, or PFAS at guideline-relevant concentrations. For any technology selection decision, use an accredited laboratory with documented detection limits.
waterTesting.verifyBody: After installing a treatment system, testing the treated water confirms it is functioning. For biological treatment, test total coliform and E. coli before and after. For chemical removal, test for specific target contaminants at the point of use. Re-test if flow rate or system behaviour changes significantly.

certification.title: Certification and Standards
certification.intro: Not all products claiming to use a given treatment technology perform equally. Certification programmes provide independent evidence that a product achieves specific contaminant reductions under defined test conditions.
certification.whyDifferTitle: Why the Same Technology Can Perform Differently
certification.whyDifferBody: A product may correctly claim to use reverse osmosis, activated carbon, or UV while performing at a fraction of a certified product's level. Reasons include inferior membrane quality, inadequate UV lamp intensity, insufficient contact time in the carbon bed, or testing under conditions more favourable than real-world use. Without independent certification, there is no way to verify manufacturer performance claims.

nsfItems.covers: Aesthetic effects — reduction of chlorine, taste, odour, and particulates. Does not certify pathogen or health-contaminant removal.
Health effects — reduction of specific health-related contaminants including lead, arsenic, cysts, and VOCs. Products must demonstrate reduction to below the applicable MCL.
Reverse osmosis systems — certifies reduction of TDS, heavy metals, nitrates, and other RO-specific contaminants.
UV systems — Class A: 4-log bacteria/viruses, 3-log Giardia/Cryptosporidium. Class B: 2-log bacteria only.

certification.whoBody: The WHO Household Water Treatment targets classify systems as Highly Protective (>=4-log bacteria, >=5-log viruses, >=4-log protozoa) or Protective (>=2-log across all categories). These targets are relevant for point-of-use treatment in settings without reliable municipal infrastructure.
redFlags: Performance claims without a cited certification body and certificate number.
Test results expressed only as percentages without specifying starting concentration, test organism, or test conditions.
Claims that a single product removes 'all contaminants' without listing which contaminants were tested.
Certification by the manufacturer's own laboratory rather than an accredited independent body.

howToChoose.title: How to Choose a Treatment System
howToChoose.intro: Choosing a water treatment system should follow the contamination profile of your specific water source, not the marketing claims of a product.
steps: Test your water. Identify which contaminants are present at what concentrations. Do not rely on assumed typical profiles.
Identify your source type. Groundwater (well, spring), surface water (river, lake), or treated municipal supply each carry different risk profiles.
Prioritise by health risk. Address microbiological contamination first. Chemical contaminants at low concentrations often pose lower immediate risk.
Match technology to contaminant. If your target contaminant is not in a method's demonstrated removal profile, do not rely on that method to remove it.
Consider operational context. Electricity availability, flow rate, maintenance capacity, and budget constrain which technologies are viable.
Build a treatment train. Most systems require multiple stages. Sequence correctly: pre-filtration before membranes, disinfection last.
Verify with certification. For any health-related claim, confirm NSF/ANSI or equivalent independent certification.
Test the treated water after installation to verify the system achieves the required reductions.
howToChoose.ctaText: The configurator walks through these steps and recommends a matched system based on your inputs.

misconceptions.title: Common Misconceptions
misconceptions.intro: Several widespread beliefs about water treatment lead to inadequate protection.

items.fact: Turbidity is not a reliable indicator of microbiological or chemical safety. Pathogen concentrations causing illness are invisible. Dissolved heavy metals, nitrates, and PFAS produce no visible signal at any concentration of health relevance.

Boiling kills pathogens but does not remove heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, PFAS, or dissolved chemicals — and may slightly concentrate them. For water with cyanobacterial blooms, boiling kills the cells but triggers cyanotoxin release, making the water more dangerous.

items.myth: More filtration stages is always better.
items.fact: Redundant or mismatched stages add cost and maintenance without improving safety. A poorly sequenced train — UV before sediment removal, for example — reduces downstream effectiveness. The goal is a well-matched sequence, not a long one.

items.myth: My filter is working because the water tastes good.
items.fact: Taste and odour thresholds are far above safety limits for most regulated contaminants. Lead, arsenic, nitrates, PFAS, and most pathogens are undetectable by taste or odour at concentrations that pose health risks.

items.myth: Reverse osmosis is the safest option for every situation.
items.fact: RO removes a wide range of contaminants but strips beneficial minerals, produces 3–4 L of wastewater per litre purified, requires electricity and regular maintenance, and is not appropriate for all deployment contexts.

items.myth: Once installed, a filter works indefinitely.
items.fact: All treatment systems require maintenance. Activated carbon saturates. UV lamp output degrades. RO membranes foul. Ceramic filters accumulate pathogens. An unmaintained system may perform worse than no treatment by creating a false sense of security.
